title: This Resume Looks Fine But Stays Invisible
thesis: A recruiter-style teardown showing why qualified experience becomes invisible when a resume misses job-description proof.
cta: Paste the job description and check your free Signal score before you apply.

sections.label: Cold open
sections.script: This resume looks professional, and that is the trap. The job description screams HubSpot, CAC, LinkedIn Ads, and lifecycle marketing. The resume whispers helped with campaigns. That is invisible experience.
sections.visual: Open on polished resume beside job description; red stamp says professional fog, then Signal points at the missing terms.

sections.label: Open loop
sections.script: In the next few minutes, we are going to do this like a recruiter would actually see it. First, I will pull the clues out of the job description. Then I will run the resume through a simple search test. Then we will find the mistake that makes a qualified person look generic. Finally, we will fix one bullet live and watch the Signal score move from 34 to 92. The point is not to make the candidate sound louder. The point is to make the existing proof easier to find.
sections.visual: Timeline cards: mistake, search test, fix, score reveal; keep the resume/JD split screen visible.

sections.label: Translate the job description
sections.script: Start with the job description, not the resume. A Demand Generation Manager posting is usually not asking for vague marketing energy. It is asking for pipeline sourced, HubSpot workflows, LinkedIn Ads, CAC analysis, lifecycle marketing, and MQL-to-SQL conversion. Those are not magic keywords. They are clues about what the hiring team wants to see quickly. If your resume has the experience but calls everything campaigns, support, and coordination, you are making the reviewer translate your background for you. Reviewers are not mind readers, and they are usually moving fast.
sections.visual: Keyword extraction board pulls terms from the JD into bright proof chips.

sections.label: Recruiter search test
sections.script: Now pretend I am searching resumes. I type HubSpot. I type CAC. I type LinkedIn Ads. I type pipeline. If this candidate has done that work, the resume should give me a clean match. Right now, it barely does. That does not mean the person is bad. It means the resume is asking me to infer too much. This is where a lot of job seekers lose attention. They think the resume is being humble and professional. To a recruiter, it can read like polite fog.
sections.visual: Recruiter search box types each keyword; weak resume returns partial or empty matches.

sections.label: Resume crime scene
sections.script: Here is the resume crime scene: helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails. That is not bad experience. It is just under-labeled experience. The bullet has HubSpot, which is useful, but it hides scope, audience, and business result. What kind of lists? For what campaigns? What happened because of the work? The evidence is wearing camouflage. A stronger resume does not need to brag. It needs to remove the camouflage so the proof is visible in a six-second skim.
sections.visual: Circle the weak bullet; Signal drops a red evidence marker beside it.

sections.label: Why it gets missed
sections.script: This is not about tricking software. The ATS is not a wizard sitting in a basement rejecting good people for sport. The practical problem is simpler: resumes get stored, searched, skimmed, compared, and forgotten. When the job description says CAC analysis and your resume says helped with campaigns, the strongest version of your experience is not on the page yet. It is stuck in your memory. A hiring team should not have to interview you just to discover the basic proof that belonged in the bullet.
sections.visual: Before bullet fades under fog overlay while JD terms stay sharp on the right.

sections.label: Live bullet fix
sections.script: Now we translate, not fabricate. The weak bullet says: helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails. A stronger version says: built HubSpot lead-scoring and LinkedIn retargeting segments that cut CAC 32% and lifted MQL-to-SQL conversion 18%. Same work category. Same candidate. Much clearer proof. Notice what changed. We added the tool, the channel, the business metric, and the result. We did not add a fake job, a fake degree, or a fake skill. That is the line Signal has to protect.
sections.visual: Before bullet slides out; after bullet locks in with HubSpot, LinkedIn Ads, CAC, and 32% highlighted.

sections.label: Score reveal
sections.script: Now the score jump makes sense. Before, the resume was sitting at 34 out of 100 because the job description language and the resume proof were not connected. After the rewrite, the example moves to 92 out of 100 because the same experience is mapped to HubSpot workflows, LinkedIn Ads, CAC analysis, lifecycle marketing, pipeline, and conversion proof. The value is not the number by itself. The value is knowing exactly what changed, and why the new version is easier for a human to understand.
sections.visual: Score meter animates 34 to 92; missing keyword chips flip from red to green.

sections.label: Trust and CTA
sections.script: That is the whole Signal promise. Find the real proof already in your work. Make it match the job description. Keep it honest. No fake jobs, no fake tools, no guaranteed outcomes, and no panic about mythical resume robots. If you are about to apply, paste the job description first and check your free Signal score. If the gap is real, then paying for a targeted resume and cover letter package makes sense. If the gap is not real, you just learned that before spending money.
sections.visual: Signal score page, trust badges, no fake experience note, and free score CTA.

weakBullets: Supported email and social campaigns across multiple channels.
Helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails.
Coordinated paid social assets with design and sales.
beforeBullet: Helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails.
afterBullet: Built HubSpot lead-scoring and LinkedIn retargeting segments that cut CAC 32% and lifted MQL-to-SQL conversion 18%.
